document_metadata.title: Facebook blocked my family. We have no idea why.
document_metadata.first_words: Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem. I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense. It began when my daughter needed to sublet her apartment. She tried to open an account on Facebook, because its Marketplace pages are the most prominent venue for such listings. After she submitted her name, email, birthdate, and a “video selfie,” which is like a live-action police mug

chunks.content: Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem. I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense. It began when my daughter needed to sublet her apartment. She tried to open an account on Facebook, because its Marketplace pages are the most prominent venue for such listings. After she submitted her name, email, birthdate, and a “video selfie,” which is like a live-action police mug shot, Facebook blocked her from having an account on the grounds that she had failed to “follow our community standards.” How could this be? She had never had a Facebook account and is a member in presumably fine standing on Instagram, which is owned by Facebook’s parent company, Meta. So she asked her roommate to try joining, and the same thing happened to her. Then my wife and I tried, with the exact same dismissal. “Your Facebook account has been permanently disabled,” said the rejection email each of us got. “You can’t request another review.” Subletting the apartment was going to be challenging if none of us could post on Facebook Marketplace.
chunks.content_original: Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem. I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense. It began when my daughter needed to sublet her apartment. She tried to open an account on Facebook, because its Marketplace pages are the most prominent venue for such listings. After she submitted her name, email, birthdate, and a “video selfie,” which is like a live-action police mug shot, Facebook blocked her from having an account on the grounds that she had failed to “follow our community standards.” How could this be? She had never had a Facebook account and is a member in presumably fine standing on Instagram, which is owned by Facebook’s parent company, Meta. So she asked her roommate to try joining, and the same thing happened to her. Then my wife and I tried, with the exact same dismissal. “Your Facebook account has been permanently disabled,” said the rejection email each of us got. “You can’t request another review.” Subletting the apartment was going to be challenging if none of us could post on Facebook Marketplace.

chunks.content: And even if we could find another way to list the apartment, the algorithmic rejection raised unsettling questions. Chief among them: Given that online platforms are now gatekeepers not just for apartments but also for jobs, credit, and so forth, are we giving up too much agency to systems that can arbitrarily shut people out with no real explanation? Things got weirder when I asked a Facebook spokeswoman about the sign-up process. For one thing, she said my denial had been a mistake. (The system may have erred in the three other instances too, but I didn’t ask her to look into their cases.) I was in fact now free to join, she said. I accept that a sign-up process like this has to be highly automated, and it’s good that Facebook has standards for whom it will accept. What bothered me was the system’s opacity and capriciousness. I wanted to know: What mistake did the system make about me? Did it determine I had run afoul of community standards by scanning my face? The spokeswoman didn’t specify what led to my rejection, but she did say I should not have been prompted to give a video selfie. She said it’s not typical for the system to request one when an account is created.
chunks.content_original: And even if we could find another way to list the apartment, the algorithmic rejection raised unsettling questions. Chief among them: Given that online platforms are now gatekeepers not just for apartments but also for jobs, credit, and so forth, are we giving up too much agency to systems that can arbitrarily shut people out with no real explanation? Things got weirder when I asked a Facebook spokeswoman about the sign-up process. For one thing, she said my denial had been a mistake. (The system may have erred in the three other instances too, but I didn’t ask her to look into their cases.) I was in fact now free to join, she said. I accept that a sign-up process like this has to be highly automated, and it’s good that Facebook has standards for whom it will accept. What bothered me was the system’s opacity and capriciousness. I wanted to know: What mistake did the system make about me? Did it determine I had run afoul of community standards by scanning my face? The spokeswoman didn’t specify what led to my rejection, but she did say I should not have been prompted to give a video selfie. She said it’s not typical for the system to request one when an account is created.

chunks.content: This was odd, because all four of us had to produce selfies. Online forums indicate that lots of people report the same thing. But if she was right, she was offering a dispiriting bit of information. Because it suggests that the registration system isn’t well designed. And Facebook is under no pressure to care very much. In my case, a human spotted an error and fixed it. But that happened because I was questioning Facebook’s media relations department. There appears to be no way for most people to appeal such decisions to a human. The usual impossibility of getting a human in the loop would trouble me less if video selfies weren’t involved. Collecting them gives Facebook high-quality images of people along with their names, birthdates, and email addresses or phone numbers. That bundle of information is valuable and potentially sensitive. So I asked what the company does with them. The response: Facebook keeps the videos for one year, but you can request that yours be deleted after 30 days. But … how? If someone has been prevented from joining Facebook, they have no account settings to adjust. I got no answer to this question.
chunks.content_original: This was odd, because all four of us had to produce selfies. Online forums indicate that lots of people report the same thing. But if she was right, she was offering a dispiriting bit of information. Because it suggests that the registration system isn’t well designed. And Facebook is under no pressure to care very much. In my case, a human spotted an error and fixed it. But that happened because I was questioning Facebook’s media relations department. There appears to be no way for most people to appeal such decisions to a human. The usual impossibility of getting a human in the loop would trouble me less if video selfies weren’t involved. Collecting them gives Facebook high-quality images of people along with their names, birthdates, and email addresses or phone numbers. That bundle of information is valuable and potentially sensitive. So I asked what the company does with them. The response: Facebook keeps the videos for one year, but you can request that yours be deleted after 30 days. But … how? If someone has been prevented from joining Facebook, they have no account settings to adjust. I got no answer to this question.

chunks.content: As absurd as this exchange was, a part of me wanted to shrug it off as a minor glitch compared with the errors automated systems have been making with far worse consequences. I thought, for example, of the inaccurate facial recognition software that has led to the arrests of innocent people or the bloodless health insurance algorithms denying people care they obviously need. But then I read “Artificial Power,” a report released in May by the AI Now Institute, an organization that examines the social impact of artificial intelligence. The report reminded me that shrugging off Facebook’s careless sign-up system would only feed the false narrative that resistance to Big Tech is futile. Much of the attention paid to AI is concerned with the utility of its applications. For example: What tasks can ChatGPT accomplish for us? What jobs might it replace? Will AI get so adept that it eventually outsmarts us? Those are important questions, to be sure.
chunks.content_original: As absurd as this exchange was, a part of me wanted to shrug it off as a minor glitch compared with the errors automated systems have been making with far worse consequences. I thought, for example, of the inaccurate facial recognition software that has led to the arrests of innocent people or the bloodless health insurance algorithms denying people care they obviously need. But then I read “Artificial Power,” a report released in May by the AI Now Institute, an organization that examines the social impact of artificial intelligence. The report reminded me that shrugging off Facebook’s careless sign-up system would only feed the false narrative that resistance to Big Tech is futile. Much of the attention paid to AI is concerned with the utility of its applications. For example: What tasks can ChatGPT accomplish for us? What jobs might it replace? Will AI get so adept that it eventually outsmarts us? Those are important questions, to be sure.

chunks.content: But the authors of the AI Now report, Kate Brennan, Amba Kak, and Sarah Myers West, point out that focusing on the “intelligence” of AI applications obscures a larger pattern operating in the background. Regardless of how well generative AI and other algorithmic technologies actually work, companies are using them to render themselves “opaque to those they are meant to serve,” the researchers write. The authors warn of “the further degradation of life for the most marginalized among us” — especially anyone who encounters an automated decision-making system that leaves people “unable to contest the determinations made by the ‘smart machine.’… These tools are often invisible to those judged by them, and inscrutable even when they are visible.” When I told West about my experience with Facebook, she said it fits with the broader context in which tech companies deploy “automated denial of access” in everything from health care to employment and housing, and we are expected to accept this as a new fact of life. “I don’t think it’s that trivial, because it’s reflective of this underlying transformation,” she said.
chunks.content_original: But the authors of the AI Now report, Kate Brennan, Amba Kak, and Sarah Myers West, point out that focusing on the “intelligence” of AI applications obscures a larger pattern operating in the background. Regardless of how well generative AI and other algorithmic technologies actually work, companies are using them to render themselves “opaque to those they are meant to serve,” the researchers write. The authors warn of “the further degradation of life for the most marginalized among us” — especially anyone who encounters an automated decision-making system that leaves people “unable to contest the determinations made by the ‘smart machine.’… These tools are often invisible to those judged by them, and inscrutable even when they are visible.” When I told West about my experience with Facebook, she said it fits with the broader context in which tech companies deploy “automated denial of access” in everything from health care to employment and housing, and we are expected to accept this as a new fact of life. “I don’t think it’s that trivial, because it’s reflective of this underlying transformation,” she said.

chunks.content: However, the goal of the AI Now report is not merely to lament this phenomenon. It’s a call to take action against it. Lawmakers in several states have tried developing legislation that would make automated decision-making systems more transparent, force them to explain themselves, and give end users “rights of due process,” as Kak puts it. There have been a few successes. “What we’ve seen time and again, though, is that at the very last moment there’s an army of Big Tech lobbyists that eventually undercut that progress,” she told me. What that means, then, is that it’s crucial to think of AI as a political problem, not just a technological one. “What’s the countervailing power?” Kak said. “Who’s coming on the other side to make the case that lawmakers need to be responding not just to industry lobbyists but to the interests of the public? And that needs to be a kind of ground-up push.”
chunks.content_original: However, the goal of the AI Now report is not merely to lament this phenomenon. It’s a call to take action against it. Lawmakers in several states have tried developing legislation that would make automated decision-making systems more transparent, force them to explain themselves, and give end users “rights of due process,” as Kak puts it. There have been a few successes. “What we’ve seen time and again, though, is that at the very last moment there’s an army of Big Tech lobbyists that eventually undercut that progress,” she told me. What that means, then, is that it’s crucial to think of AI as a political problem, not just a technological one. “What’s the countervailing power?” Kak said. “Who’s coming on the other side to make the case that lawmakers need to be responding not just to industry lobbyists but to the interests of the public? And that needs to be a kind of ground-up push.”

chunks.content: There are signs that such a groundswell is possible. As one example, the AI Now researchers highlight the National Nurses United union, which spelled out an AI Bill of Rights for patients last year. In Pennsylvania this year, a local chapter of the Service Employees International Union worked with Governor Josh Shapiro to get state workers involved in overseeing how generative AI tools are implemented in government. At the federal level, an unwillingness to let AI unfold purely on Silicon Valley’s terms may be a rare point of consensus among politicians on the left and the right. Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts and Republican Senator Josh Hawley of Missouri have both called for breaking up tech companies. The first version of President Trump’s massive domestic policy bill that passed the House last month included a 10-year moratorium on state regulation of AI, but the Senate voted 99-1 to kill that provision.
chunks.content_original: There are signs that such a groundswell is possible. As one example, the AI Now researchers highlight the National Nurses United union, which spelled out an AI Bill of Rights for patients last year. In Pennsylvania this year, a local chapter of the Service Employees International Union worked with Governor Josh Shapiro to get state workers involved in overseeing how generative AI tools are implemented in government. At the federal level, an unwillingness to let AI unfold purely on Silicon Valley’s terms may be a rare point of consensus among politicians on the left and the right. Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts and Republican Senator Josh Hawley of Missouri have both called for breaking up tech companies. The first version of President Trump’s massive domestic policy bill that passed the House last month included a 10-year moratorium on state regulation of AI, but the Senate voted 99-1 to kill that provision.

chunks.content: As Kak puts it, tech companies’ extreme wealth and power are “contingent on, essentially, a public that is submissive, passive.” What does that mean for everyday users of technology? If you need to sublet an apartment, I don’t have a great alternative to Facebook Marketplace to suggest at the moment. But I do know that it would help to make such platforms more accountable to the public. And we need to support the flourishing of many such alternatives — ones that don’t put you at the mercy of a system that amasses video selfies but has no face of its own.

If you need to sublet an apartment, I don’t have a great alternative to Facebook Marketplace to suggest at the moment. But I do know that it would help to make such platforms more accountable to the public. And we need to support the flourishing of many such alternatives — ones that don’t put you at the mercy of a system that amasses video selfies but has no face of its own.

markdown_content:
Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem.
I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense.
It began when my daughter needed to sublet her apartment. She tried to open an account on Facebook, because its Marketplace pages are the most prominent venue for such listings. After she submitted her name, email, birthdate, and a “video selfie,” which is like a live-action police mug shot, Facebook blocked her from having an account on the grounds that she had failed to “follow our community standards.”
How could this be? She had never had a Facebook account and is a member in presumably fine standing on Instagram, which is owned by Facebook’s parent company, Meta. So she asked her roommate to try joining, and the same thing happened to her. Then my wife and I tried, with the exact same dismissal.
“Your Facebook account has been permanently disabled,” said the rejection email each of us got. “You can’t request another review.”
Subletting the apartment was going to be challenging if none of us could post on Facebook Marketplace.
And even if we could find another way to list the apartment, the algorithmic rejection raised unsettling questions. Chief among them: Given that online platforms are now gatekeepers not just for apartments but also for jobs, credit, and so forth, are we giving up too much agency to systems that can arbitrarily shut people out with no real explanation?
Things got weirder when I asked a Facebook spokeswoman about the sign-up process.
For one thing, she said my denial had been a mistake. (The system may have erred in the three other instances too, but I didn’t ask her to look into their cases.) I was in fact now free to join, she said.
I accept that a sign-up process like this has to be highly automated, and it’s good that Facebook has standards for whom it will accept. What bothered me was the system’s opacity and capriciousness. I wanted to know: What mistake did the system make about me? Did it determine I had run afoul of community standards by scanning my face?
The spokeswoman didn’t specify what led to my rejection, but she did say I should not have been prompted to give a video selfie. She said it’s not typical for the system to request one when an account is created.
This was odd, because all four of us had to produce selfies. Online forums indicate that lots of people report the same thing. But if she was right, she was offering a dispiriting bit of information. Because it suggests that the registration system isn’t well designed. And Facebook is under no pressure to care very much.
In my case, a human spotted an error and fixed it. But that happened because I was questioning Facebook’s media relations department. There appears to be no way for most people to appeal such decisions to a human.
The usual impossibility of getting a human in the loop would trouble me less if video selfies weren’t involved. Collecting them gives Facebook high-quality images of people along with their names, birthdates, and email addresses or phone numbers. That bundle of information is valuable and potentially sensitive. So I asked what the company does with them.
The response: Facebook keeps the videos for one year, but you can request that yours be deleted after 30 days.
But … how? If someone has been prevented from joining Facebook, they have no account settings to adjust. I got no answer to this question.
As absurd as this exchange was, a part of me wanted to shrug it off as a minor glitch compared with the errors automated systems have been making with far worse consequences. I thought, for example, of the inaccurate facial recognition software that has led to the arrests of innocent people or the bloodless health insurance algorithms denying people care they obviously need.
But then I read “Artificial Power,” a report released in May by the AI Now Institute, an organization that examines the social impact of artificial intelligence. The report reminded me that shrugging off Facebook’s careless sign-up system would only feed the false narrative that resistance to Big Tech is futile.
Much of the attention paid to AI is concerned with the utility of its applications. For example: What tasks can ChatGPT accomplish for us? What jobs might it replace? Will AI get so adept that it eventually outsmarts us? Those are important questions, to be sure.
But the authors of the AI Now report, Kate Brennan, Amba Kak, and Sarah Myers West, point out that focusing on the “intelligence” of AI applications obscures a larger pattern operating in the background. Regardless of how well generative AI and other algorithmic technologies actually work, companies are using them to render themselves “opaque to those they are meant to serve,” the researchers write.
The authors warn of “the further degradation of life for the most marginalized among us” — especially anyone who encounters an automated decision-making system that leaves people “unable to contest the determinations made by the ‘smart machine.’… These tools are often invisible to those judged by them, and inscrutable even when they are visible.”
When I told West about my experience with Facebook, she said it fits with the broader context in which tech companies deploy “automated denial of access” in everything from health care to employment and housing, and we are expected to accept this as a new fact of life. “I don’t think it’s that trivial, because it’s reflective of this underlying transformation,” she said.
However, the goal of the AI Now report is not merely to lament this phenomenon. It’s a call to take action against it.
Lawmakers in several states have tried developing legislation that would make automated decision-making systems more transparent, force them to explain themselves, and give end users “rights of due process,” as Kak puts it. There have been a few successes. “What we’ve seen time and again, though, is that at the very last moment there’s an army of Big Tech lobbyists that eventually undercut that progress,” she told me.
What that means, then, is that it’s crucial to think of AI as a political problem, not just a technological one. “What’s the countervailing power?” Kak said. “Who’s coming on the other side to make the case that lawmakers need to be responding not just to industry lobbyists but to the interests of the public? And that needs to be a kind of ground-up push.”
There are signs that such a groundswell is possible. As one example, the AI Now researchers highlight the National Nurses United union, which spelled out an AI Bill of Rights for patients last year. In Pennsylvania this year, a local chapter of the Service Employees International Union worked with Governor Josh Shapiro to get state workers involved in overseeing how generative AI tools are implemented in government.
At the federal level, an unwillingness to let AI unfold purely on Silicon Valley’s terms may be a rare point of consensus among politicians on the left and the right. Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts and Republican Senator Josh Hawley of Missouri have both called for breaking up tech companies. The first version of President Trump’s massive domestic policy bill that passed the House last month included a 10-year moratorium on state regulation of AI, but the Senate voted 99-1 to kill that provision.
As Kak puts it, tech companies’ extreme wealth and power are “contingent on, essentially, a public that is submissive, passive.” What does that mean for everyday users of technology? If you need to sublet an apartment, I don’t have a great alternative to Facebook Marketplace to suggest at the moment.
But I do know that it would help to make such platforms more accountable to the public. And we need to support the flourishing of many such alternatives — ones that don’t put you at the mercy of a system that amasses video selfies but has no face of its own.

Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem.
I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense.
It began when my daughter needed to sublet her apartment. She tried to open an account on Facebook, because its Marketplace pages are the most prominent venue for such listings. After she submitted her name, email, birthdate, and a “video selfie,” which is like a live-action police mug shot, Facebook blocked her from having an account on the grounds that she had failed to “follow our community standards.”
How could this be? She had never had a Facebook account and is a member in presumably fine standing on Instagram, which is owned by Facebook’s parent company, Meta. So she asked her roommate to try joining, and the same thing happened to her. Then my wife and I tried, with the exact same dismissal.
“Your Facebook account has been permanently disabled,” said the rejection email each of us got. “You can’t request another review.”
Subletting the apartment was going to be challenging if none of us could post on Facebook Marketplace.
And even if we could find another way to list the apartment, the algorithmic rejection raised unsettling questions. Chief among them: Given that online platforms are now gatekeepers not just for apartments but also for jobs, credit, and so forth, are we giving up too much agency to systems that can arbitrarily shut people out with no real explanation?
Things got weirder when I asked a Facebook spokeswoman about the sign-up process.
For one thing, she said my denial had been a mistake. (The system may have erred in the three other instances too, but I didn’t ask her to look into their cases.) I was in fact now free to join, she said.
I accept that a sign-up process like this has to be highly automated, and it’s good that Facebook has standards for whom it will accept. What bothered me was the system’s opacity and capriciousness. I wanted to know: What mistake did the system make about me? Did it determine I had run afoul of community standards by scanning my face?
The spokeswoman didn’t specify what led to my rejection, but she did say I should not have been prompted to give a video selfie. She said it’s not typical for the system to request one when an account is created.
This was odd, because all four of us had to produce selfies. Online forums indicate that lots of people report the same thing. But if she was right, she was offering a dispiriting bit of information. Because it suggests that the registration system isn’t well designed. And Facebook is under no pressure to care very much.
In my case, a human spotted an error and fixed it. But that happened because I was questioning Facebook’s media relations department. There appears to be no way for most people to appeal such decisions to a human.
The usual impossibility of getting a human in the loop would trouble me less if video selfies weren’t involved. Collecting them gives Facebook high-quality images of people along with their names, birthdates, and email addresses or phone numbers. That bundle of information is valuable and potentially sensitive. So I asked what the company does with them.
The response: Facebook keeps the videos for one year, but you can request that yours be deleted after 30 days.
But … how? If someone has been prevented from joining Facebook, they have no account settings to adjust. I got no answer to this question.
As absurd as this exchange was, a part of me wanted to shrug it off as a minor glitch compared with the errors automated systems have been making with far worse consequences. I thought, for example, of the inaccurate facial recognition software that has led to the arrests of innocent people or the bloodless health insurance algorithms denying people care they obviously need.
But then I read “Artificial Power,” a report released in May by the AI Now Institute, an organization that examines the social impact of artificial intelligence. The report reminded me that shrugging off Facebook’s careless sign-up system would only feed the false narrative that resistance to Big Tech is futile.
Much of the attention paid to AI is concerned with the utility of its applications. For example: What tasks can ChatGPT accomplish for us? What jobs might it replace? Will AI get so adept that it eventually outsmarts us? Those are important questions, to be sure.
But the authors of the AI Now report, Kate Brennan, Amba Kak, and Sarah Myers West, point out that focusing on the “intelligence” of AI applications obscures a larger pattern operating in the background. Regardless of how well generative AI and other algorithmic technologies actually work, companies are using them to render themselves “opaque to those they are meant to serve,” the researchers write.
The authors warn of “the further degradation of life for the most marginalized among us” — especially anyone who encounters an automated decision-making system that leaves people “unable to contest the determinations made by the ‘smart machine.’… These tools are often invisible to those judged by them, and inscrutable even when they are visible.”
When I told West about my experience with Facebook, she said it fits with the broader context in which tech companies deploy “automated denial of access” in everything from health care to employment and housing, and we are expected to accept this as a new fact of life. “I don’t think it’s that trivial, because it’s reflective of this underlying transformation,” she said.
However, the goal of the AI Now report is not merely to lament this phenomenon. It’s a call to take action against it.
Lawmakers in several states have tried developing legislation that would make automated decision-making systems more transparent, force them to explain themselves, and give end users “rights of due process,” as Kak puts it. There have been a few successes. “What we’ve seen time and again, though, is that at the very last moment there’s an army of Big Tech lobbyists that eventually undercut that progress,” she told me.
What that means, then, is that it’s crucial to think of AI as a political problem, not just a technological one. “What’s the countervailing power?” Kak said. “Who’s coming on the other side to make the case that lawmakers need to be responding not just to industry lobbyists but to the interests of the public? And that needs to be a kind of ground-up push.”
There are signs that such a groundswell is possible. As one example, the AI Now researchers highlight the National Nurses United union, which spelled out an AI Bill of Rights for patients last year. In Pennsylvania this year, a local chapter of the Service Employees International Union worked with Governor Josh Shapiro to get state workers involved in overseeing how generative AI tools are implemented in government.
At the federal level, an unwillingness to let AI unfold purely on Silicon Valley’s terms may be a rare point of consensus among politicians on the left and the right. Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts and Republican Senator Josh Hawley of Missouri have both called for breaking up tech companies. The first version of President Trump’s massive domestic policy bill that passed the House last month included a 10-year moratorium on state regulation of AI, but the Senate voted 99-1 to kill that provision.
As Kak puts it, tech companies’ extreme wealth and power are “contingent on, essentially, a public that is submissive, passive.” What does that mean for everyday users of technology? If you need to sublet an apartment, I don’t have a great alternative to Facebook Marketplace to suggest at the moment.
But I do know that it would help to make such platforms more accountable to the public. And we need to support the flourishing of many such alternatives — ones that don’t put you at the mercy of a system that amasses video selfies but has no face of its own.

Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem.

I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense.

That bundle of information is valuable and potentially sensitive. So I asked what the company does with them.

The response: Facebook keeps the videos for one year, but you can request that yours be deleted after 30 days.

But … how? If someone has been prevented from joining Facebook, they have no account settings to adjust. I got no answer to this question.

As absurd as this exchange was, a part of me wanted to shrug it off as a minor glitch compared with the errors automated systems have been making with far worse consequences. I thought, for example, of the inaccurate facial recognition software that has led to the arrests of innocent people or the bloodless health insurance algorithms denying people care they obviously need.

But then I read “Artificial Power,” a report released in May by the AI Now Institute, an organization that examines the social impact of artificial intelligence. The report reminded me that shrugging off Facebook’s careless sign-up system would only feed the false narrative that resistance to Big Tech is futile.

Much of the attention paid to AI is concerned with the utility of its applications. For example: What tasks can ChatGPT accomplish for us? What jobs might it replace? Will AI get so adept that it eventually outsmarts us? Those are important questions, to be sure.

But the authors of the AI Now report, Kate Brennan, Amba Kak, and Sarah Myers West, point out that focusing on the “intelligence” of AI applications obscures a larger pattern operating in the background. Regardless of how well generative AI and other algorithmic technologies actually work, companies are using them to render themselves “opaque to those they are meant to serve,” the researchers write.

The authors warn of “the further degradation of life for the most marginalized among us” — especially anyone who encounters an automated decision-making system that leaves people “unable to contest the determinations made by the ‘smart machine.’… These tools are often invisible to those judged by them, and inscrutable even when they are visible.”

When I told West about my experience with Facebook, she said it fits with the broader context in which tech companies deploy “automated denial of access” in everything from health care to employment and housing, and we are expected to accept this as a new fact of life. “I don’t think it’s that trivial, because it’s reflective of this underlying transformation,” she said.

However, the goal of the AI Now report is not merely to lament this phenomenon. It’s a call to take action against it.

Lawmakers in several states have tried developing legislation that would make automated decision-making systems more transparent, force them to explain themselves, and give end users “rights of due process,” as Kak puts it. There have been a few successes. “What we’ve seen time and again, though, is that at the very last moment there’s an army of Big Tech lobbyists that eventually undercut that progress,” she told me.

What that means, then, is that it’s crucial to think of AI as a political problem, not just a technological one. “What’s the countervailing power?” Kak said. “Who’s coming on the other side to make the case that lawmakers need to be responding not just to industry lobbyists but to the interests of the public? And that needs to be a kind of ground-up push.”

There are signs that such a groundswell is possible. As one example, the AI Now researchers highlight the National Nurses United union, which spelled out an AI Bill of Rights for patients last year. In Pennsylvania this year, a local chapter of the Service Employees International Union worked with Governor Josh Shapiro to get state workers involved in overseeing how generative AI tools are implemented in government.

At the federal level, an unwillingness to let AI unfold purely on Silicon Valley’s terms may be a rare point of consensus among politicians on the left and the right. Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts and Republican Senator Josh Hawley of Missouri have both called for breaking up tech companies. The first version of President Trump’s massive domestic policy bill that passed the House last month included a 10-year moratorium on state regulation of AI, but the Senate voted 99-1 to kill that provision.

As Kak puts it, tech companies’ extreme wealth and power are “contingent on, essentially, a public that is submissive, passive.” What does that mean for everyday users of technology? If you need to sublet an apartment, I don’t have a great alternative to Facebook Marketplace to suggest at the moment.

But I do know that it would help to make such platforms more accountable to the public. And we need to support the flourishing of many such alternatives — ones that don’t put you at the mercy of a system that amasses video selfies but has no face of its own.

cleaned_content:
Sometimes, a small thing reveals volumes about a big problem.

I encountered one of these things recently. It turned out to be an example of how corporations are delegating more and more authority to machines in the name of innovation and at the average person’s expense.

It began when my daughter needed to sublet her apartment. She tried to open an account on Facebook, because its Marketplace pages are the most prominent venue for such listings. After she submitted her name, email, birthdate, and a “video selfie,” which is like a live-action police mug shot, Facebook blocked her from having an account on the grounds that she had failed to “follow our community standards.”

How could this be? She had never had a Facebook account and is a member in presumably fine standing on Instagram, which is owned by Facebook’s parent company, Meta. So she asked her roommate to try joining, and the same thing happened to her. Then my wife and I tried, with the exact same dismissal.

“Your Facebook account has been permanently disabled,” said the rejection email each of us got. “You can’t request another review.”

Subletting the apartment was going to be challenging if none of us could post on Facebook Marketplace.

And even if we could find another way to list the apartment, the algorithmic rejection raised unsettling questions. Chief among them: Given that online platforms are now gatekeepers not just for apartments but also for jobs, credit, and so forth, are we giving up too much agency to systems that can arbitrarily shut people out with no real explanation?

Things got weirder when I asked a Facebook spokeswoman about the sign-up process.

For one thing, she said my denial had been a mistake. (The system may have erred in the three other instances too, but I didn’t ask her to look into their cases.) I was in fact now free to join, she said.

I accept that a sign-up process like this has to be highly automated, and it’s good that Facebook has standards for whom it will accept. What bothered me was the system’s opacity and capriciousness. I wanted to know: What mistake did the system make about me? Did it determine I had run afoul of community standards by scanning my face?

The spokeswoman didn’t specify what led to my rejection, but she did say I should not have been prompted to give a video selfie. She said it’s not typical for the system to request one when an account is created.

This was odd, because all four of us had to produce selfies. Online forums indicate that lots of people report the same thing. But if she was right, she was offering a dispiriting bit of information. Because it suggests that the registration system isn’t well designed. And Facebook is under no pressure to care very much.

In my case, a human spotted an error and fixed it. But that happened because I was questioning Facebook’s media relations department. There appears to be no way for most people to appeal such decisions to a human.

The usual impossibility of getting a human in the loop would trouble me less if video selfies weren’t involved. Collecting them gives Facebook high-quality images of people along with their names, birthdates, and email addresses or phone numbers. That bundle of information is valuable and potentially sensitive. So I asked what the company does with them.

The response: Facebook keeps the videos for one year, but you can request that yours be deleted after 30 days.

But … how? If someone has been prevented from joining Facebook, they have no account settings to adjust. I got no answer to this question.

As absurd as this exchange was, a part of me wanted to shrug it off as a minor glitch compared with the errors automated systems have been making with far worse consequences. When I told West about my experience with Facebook, she said it fits with the broader context in which tech companies deploy “automated denial of access” in everything from health care to employment and housing, and we are expected to accept this as a new fact of life. “I don’t think it’s that trivial, because it’s reflective of this underlying transformation,” she said.

There have been a few successes. “What we’ve seen time and again, though, is that at the very last moment there’s an army of Big Tech lobbyists that eventually undercut that progress,” she told me.

What that means, then, is that it’s crucial to think of AI as a political problem, not just a technological one. “What’s the countervailing power?” Kak said. “Who’s coming on the other side to make the case that lawmakers need to be responding not just to industry lobbyists but to the interests of the public? And that needs to be a kind of ground-up push.”

There are signs that such a groundswell is possible. As one example, the AI Now researchers highlight the National Nurses United union, which spelled out an AI Bill of Rights for patients last year. In Pennsylvania this year, a local chapter of the Service Employees International Union worked with Governor Josh Shapiro to get state workers involved in overseeing how generative AI tools are implemented in government.
